title: There May Be Three Times More Insect Species Than We Realized
author: Jake Currie
text: Subscribe to skip adsAdvertisementZoologyThere May Be Three Times More Insect Species Than We RealizedThe overwhelming majority are unknown to scienceJCBy Jake Currie3:00 PM CDT on June 29, 2026Share on FacebookShare on X (formerly Twitter)Share on RedditShare on EmailShare on BlueskyWith their highly adaptable segmented body plans, small sizes, and quick generation times, insects have shown a remarkable ability to adapt to almost any environment. In fact, they’re the most diverse and abundant animal groups on Earth, living on every continent and biome. But we still don’t know how many species there are. Current estimates range around 5.5 million, but only around 1.2 million are known to science. And according to a study published today in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, there could be a lot more than we previously thought.Subscribe to skip adsFeatured VideoPowered By 10 Experts Say They’ve Discovered Liquid Water Hidden Beneath the Surface of MarsShareNextStayEntomologists from Cornell University arrived at that conclusion after taking a genetic census of parasitoid wasps in Costa Rica’s Área de Conservación Guanacaste (ACG), a massive nature preserve in the northeastern corner of the country. These wasps lay their eggs in caterpillars, and when they hatch, the larvae devour the caterpillars from the inside and emerge as adults. As part of their intense sampling, researchers collected new adults as they emerged from their hosts and used tent traps to capture others, along with plenty of non-wasp insects. Read more: “Why I Traveled the World Hunting for Mutant Bugs”Subscribe to skip adsAdvertisement
